title: DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open Finding Trend by Provider

compliance.assurance_rationale: DORA Art.28 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.40: DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open Finding Trend by Provider.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.3.40' running on sourcetype snow:problem and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=itsm` `sourcetype="snow:problem"` (vendor_id, state, opened_at); `index=vuln` `sourcetype="tenable:vuln"` (dns_name, plugin_id) joined to vendor via CMDB
app: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928), Tenable Add-On for Splunk (Splunkbase 4060)
spl:
index=itsm sourcetype="snow:problem" vendor_id=* state!="Closed" earliest=-180d
| timechart span=30d count by vendor_id limit_use_times=0
| untable _time vendor_id count
| eventstats avg(count) as baseline by vendor_id
| eval spike=if(count > baseline*1.5 AND count>5,1,0)
| where spike=1
| table _time, vendor_id, count, baseline
description: A static risk score hides deteriorating behavior. Trending open audit findings, security scan failures, and SLA breaches per provider surfaces emerging third-party risk for the register narrative and escalation triggers.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for DORA compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm, vuln and sourcetypes snow:problem, tenable:vuln. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Require `vendor_id` on problems; (2) tune multiplier for low-volume vendors; (3) correlate with contract renewal dates; (4) feed TPR committee; (5) document remediation in GRC—not Splunk narratives with PII. Indexes required: `index=itsm`, `index=vuln`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:problem`, `sourcetype=tenable:vuln`.
visualization: Line chart (count by vendor), Table (spikes), Single value (vendors spiking).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Remediation timeline within contractual SLA** — Open findings within the agreed remediation timeline per the service contract are being addressed, not neglected.

2. **Finding volume from expanded scope** — Increases in open findings after assessment scope expansion represent improved visibility, not deteriorating posture.

3. **Severity recalibration effects** — Methodology changes that increase severity ratings for existing findings create apparent deterioration without new issues.

4. **Consolidated findings across services** — Multiple findings against different services from the same provider may be addressed through a single remediation programme.

5. **Informational findings in density calculation** — Low-risk informational findings inflating density metrics should be separated from risk-bearing findings in analysis.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`dora_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by process owner, approval reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries quarterly per the ICT risk management framework review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.28`. The control objective is: "Evidence that DORA Art.28 (ICT third-party risk) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.3.40: DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open Finding Trend by Provider." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:problem`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Require `vendor_id` on problems. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** tune multiplier for low-volume vendors. Exclusions should be time-bound and logged — a permanent exclusion baked into the search query becomes invisible over time and future owners cannot tell it was intentional.

• **Hint 3:** correlate with contract renewal dates. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** feed TPR committee. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 5:** document remediation in GRC—not Splunk narratives with PII. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

`eventstats` attaches a group-level aggregate to every row in the result set without collapsing rows. For "DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open Finding Trend by Provider" this gives each event per-peer context — a row can be compared to its group's median, average, or quantiles without losing the row-level fields the detection relies on.

For "DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open Finding Trend by Provider," the aggregation exposes temporal structure that a single point-in-time reading cannot — the compliance question is about behaviour over a period, not at an instant.

The grouping `by vendor_id, limit_use_times=0` is the analytical unit — the aggregate is computed within each distinct combination. The primary dimension here is `vendor_id` — the detection's finding is effectively 'a vendor_id whose behaviour diverges from its own historical baseline or its peer group,' not a population-wide threshold. A common mistake is grouping by a field that has cardinality near 1:1 with events, which produces a group-per-event and makes the window stat trivially equal to the event value.

Memory sensitivity: `eventstats` must hold the entire result set in memory to compute per-row aggregates. For searches returning >1M events, this can OOM the search head. If you see `fatal=true` in `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd` or mrsparkle restarts, reduce the base-search time window or pre-aggregate before `eventstats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.3.40: DORA ICT Third-Party Risk Register — Issue Density and Open ]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -180d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.3.40,reg=DORA
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:problem earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `_time`, `baseline`, `count`, `spike`, `vendor_id`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:problem earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table _time, baseline, count, spike, vendor_id
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `count`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(count) as p50 perc90(count) as p90 perc99(count) as p99 max(count) as hi
```

The current threshold `5` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.3.40: DORA evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Line Chart (`count by vendor`):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Drill-through table (`spikes`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 3 — Single-value KPI (`vendors spiking`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'DORA current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**DORA-specific failure modes:**

• Third-party ICT risk register (Art. 28): DORA requires a Register of Information listing every critical ICT third-party service provider. Detections touching supplier risk must reference this register; if a supplier appears in telemetry but not in the Register, that is a Register-completeness finding, not just a technical anomaly.

• Testing cadence (Art. 24-27): DORA's TLPT (Threat-Led Penetration Testing) is required every 3 years for significant institutions. Detections measuring resilience testing must differentiate TLPT (supervisor-led) from self-assessment — mixing them dilutes the evidence.

• Incident classification thresholds (Art. 18): DORA uses specific criteria for 'major' vs 'significant' incidents — client impact, duration, data volume. Detections must classify against these specific thresholds, which differ from GDPR breach criteria; a single event can be major-DORA but not a personal-data breach.

• Resilience testing record retention (Art. 27): results of digital operational resilience tests must be retained. Align log retention to match — typically 5+ years. Verify `frozenTimePeriodInSecs >= 157680000` on any index named by a DORA resilience-testing UC.
grandmaExplanation: We a static risk score hides deteriorating behavior.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Configure a test provider risk register with 15 open findings (exceeding the 10-finding density threshold); verify the density search flags the excessive finding count for management review.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Configure a test provider risk register with 5 open findings (below threshold); verify no density alert fires.

evidence: Issue density evidence showing finding counts by severity, age analysis, remediation progress, and trend reports for management body oversight.
exclusions: Findings within contractual remediation SLA; finding increases from expanded assessment scope (improved visibility); informational findings below risk threshold excluded from density calculations.
